title: Interfaces

publication.changeSummary: Language-specific source-linked draft lesson for Interfaces; independent technical, source, pedagogy, accessibility, and runnable-example review remains required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: Learn Java; accessed 2026-07; Interfaces, Inheritance, Polymorphism, and composition guidance; atomic topic: Interfaces
sources.claim: A Java interface defines a focused behavioral contract that unrelated classes can implement and clients can depend on without knowing concrete representation.

sources.locator: Java Language Specification, Java SE 21; Java SE 21; JLS chapters 8-9 and §§15.12.2, 15.12.4: inheritance, interfaces, overload selection, and dispatch; atomic topic: Interfaces
sources.claim: A Java interface defines a focused behavioral contract that unrelated classes can implement and clients can depend on without knowing concrete representation.

canonicalExplanation.summary: A Java interface defines a focused behavioral contract that unrelated classes can implement and clients can depend on without knowing concrete representation.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: FileNotifier and MemoryNotifier implement one Notifier interface, letting a service test against the memory implementation without branching on concrete types. Your goal is to design and use a focused interface with at least two conforming implementations. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Constructors and invariants; Classes, fields and methods. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language-specific operational model
sections.body: A Java interface defines a focused behavioral contract that unrelated classes can implement and clients can depend on without knowing concrete representation. This model is applied through the declared Java: Beginner to Intermediate behavior rather than a language-neutral slogan.

sections.heading: Boundary and failure model
sections.body: Default methods can create conflicts, interface constants are public static final, contracts include semantics beyond method signatures, and a broad interface can still couple clients tightly. The learner must reject this documented misconception: Any classes with matching method names already satisfy a Java interface without declaring implements or honoring semantic behavior.

includes: Define behavioral contracts and implement them with unrelated classes.
Design and use a focused interface with at least two conforming implementations.
excludes: Default methods can create conflicts, interface constants are public static final, contracts include semantics beyond method signatures, and a broad interface can still couple clients tightly.

outcomes: Design and use a focused interface with at least two conforming implementations.

examples.title: Interfaces: direct language case
examples.situation: FileNotifier and MemoryNotifier implement one Notifier interface, letting a service test against the memory implementation without branching on concrete types.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the concrete Java: Beginner to Intermediate state or tool result: FileNotifier and MemoryNotifier implement one Notifier interface, letting a service test against the memory implementation without branching on concrete types.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.

Step 3 — Verify the result with this correction: Declare implements and satisfy both the interface signatures and documented behavioral contract; clients should depend on the smallest coherent interface.
examples.result: The example meets the atomic outcome because Declare implements and satisfy both the interface signatures and documented behavioral contract; clients should depend on the smallest coherent interface.

examples.title: Interfaces: boundary and transfer case
examples.situation: A default method collision from two parent interfaces is resolved explicitly in the implementing class rather than relying on declaration order.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Name what changed from the direct case: A default method collision from two parent interfaces is resolved explicitly in the implementing class rather than relying on declaration order.

Step 2 — Keep the language boundary explicit: Default methods can create conflicts, interface constants are public static final, contracts include semantics beyond method signatures, and a broad interface can still couple clients tightly.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting but incorrect rule: Any classes with matching method names already satisfy a Java interface without declaring implements or honoring semantic behavior.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Design and use a focused interface with at least two conforming implementations.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: Any classes with matching method names already satisfy a Java interface without declaring implements or honoring semantic behavior.
misconceptions.correction: Declare implements and satisfy both the interface signatures and documented behavioral contract; clients should depend on the smallest coherent interface.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Predict both examples and identify the first observable contradiction in this belief: Any classes with matching method names already satisfy a Java interface without declaring implements or honoring semantic behavior.

analogy.example: a plug-and-socket system in which contracts describe compatible behavior while components hide their implementation can introduce Interfaces, after the canonical Java: Beginner to Intermediate rule is stated first.
limitations: overload resolution, method hiding, constructor order, and multiple interface defaults require Java-specific rules

recap.summary: You should now be able to design and use a focused interface with at least two conforming implementations. Keep this boundary in view: Default methods can create conflicts, interface constants are public static final, contracts include semantics beyond method signatures, and a broad interface can still couple clients tightly.

Why is this belief incorrect: Any classes with matching method names already satisfy a Java interface without declaring implements or honoring semantic behavior.
